channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: The Cosmic Web
item.description:
If you could see the universe from outside, it would look like a sponge – a vast three-dimensional network of filaments, sheets, and dense knots surrounding enormous empty voids.

How the web formed

The cosmic microwave background shows that the early universe was almost perfectly uniform, but not quite. Tiny density fluctuations – about one part in 100,000 – were present 380,000 years after the Big Bang. These fluctuations were the seeds.

item.title: The Dark Sector
item.description:
Ninety-five percent of the universe is made of stuff we cannot see, cannot touch, and do not understand. This is not a metaphor. It is the quantitative result of decades of independent observations.

Dark matter: the evidence

Dark matter makes itself known through gravity. The evidence comes from multiple independent sources, and no single one would be decisive, but together they are overwhelming.

Galaxy rotation curves. In the 1970s, Vera Rubin and Kent Ford measured the orbital speeds of stars in spiral galaxies. Stars far from the galactic center orbit just as fast as those near it. Newtonian gravity predicts speeds should fall off with distance from the center (as they do in the solar system). They do not. The flat rotation curves imply a large halo of invisible mass surrounding each galaxy.

item.title: What the Universe Is Made Of
item.description:
Everything you have ever seen, touched, or tasted is made of protons, neutrons, and electrons. This ordinary matter – baryonic matter – accounts for every star, every planet, every grain of dust, every living thing. It is also less than five percent of the universe.

The visible inventory

Start with stars. A star is a sphere of plasma held together by gravity, generating energy through nuclear fusion. Stars range from dim red dwarfs (the most common) to blue supergiants thousands of times more luminous than the Sun. Fusion in stellar cores builds the periodic table: hydrogen fuses into helium, helium into carbon, carbon into oxygen, and so on up to iron. Everything heavier than iron is forged in supernova explosions.
